quiz_questions.question_text: A 20 kg box on a horizontal surface has μ_s = 0.5. What minimum horizontal force is needed to start it moving? (g = 10 m/s²)
A 10 kg block slides on a surface with μ_k = 0.3. What is the kinetic friction force? (g = 10 m/s²)
A 5 kg block rests on a horizontal surface. A 40 N force pushes it at 30° below horizontal. What is the normal force? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: N = mg + F sin30° = 50 + 40(0.5) = 50 + 20 = 70 N. The downward component of the push increases the normal force.

quiz_questions.question_text: At what angle does a block begin to slide on an inclined plane if μ_s = 0.577?
quiz_questions.explanation: The block slides when tanθ = μ_s. θ = arctan(0.577) = 30°.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 15 kg box is pulled across a floor with μ_k = 0.25 by a horizontal force of 60 N. What is its acceleration? (g = 10 m/s²)
A block on a 45° frictionless incline weighs 100 N. What is the normal force on the block?
A 4 kg block is pressed against a vertical wall by a 60 N horizontal force. If μ_s = 0.5, does the block slide? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: Yes, because weight (40 N) exceeds max static friction (30 N)
Yes, because the applied force is horizontal
No, because friction (30 N) supports the weight (40 N)
No, because the applied force exceeds the weight

quiz_questions.explanation: N = 60 N (from the push). Max static friction = μ_s N = 0.5(60) = 30 N. Weight = 4(10) = 40 N > 30 N. The block slides down.

quiz_questions.question_text: A block is pulled up a 30° incline at constant velocity by a force parallel to the incline. If the block weighs 80 N and μ_k = 0.2, what is the pulling force?
quiz_questions.explanation: At constant velocity: F = mg sinθ + μ_k mg cosθ = 80(sin30°) + 0.2(80)(cos30°) = 40 + 0.2(69.3) = 40 + 13.9 = 53.9 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 50 N force is applied at 37° above horizontal to a 10 kg block on a surface with μ_k = 0.3. What is the acceleration? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: N = mg - F sinθ = 100 - 50(0.6) = 70 N. f_k = 0.3(70) = 21 N. F_horiz = 50 cos37° = 40 N. a = (40-21)/10 = 19/10 = 1.9 m/s². Approximately 1.58 m/s² with more precise trig values.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two blocks stacked vertically (4 kg on top of 6 kg) are on a frictionless floor. The coefficient of static friction between the blocks is 0.4. What maximum horizontal force can be applied to the bottom block? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: Max acceleration for top block: a_max = μ_s g = 0.4(10) = 4 m/s². F_max = (m₁+m₂)a_max = 10(4) = 40 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is static friction?

options.text: The friction that causes objects to accelerate
The friction force that prevents an object from starting to move, adjusting up to a maximum value of μ_s × N
The friction between surfaces separated by a lubricant
The friction force on a moving object

quiz_questions.explanation: Static friction prevents relative motion between surfaces in contact. It varies from 0 up to f_s,max = μ_s N, matching the applied force until the maximum is exceeded.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is kinetic friction?

options.text: The friction force that acts on surfaces sliding relative to each other: f_k = μ_k × N
The friction between two perfectly smooth surfaces
The friction force that prevents motion from starting

quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic friction acts between surfaces in relative motion: f_k = μ_k N, where μ_k is typically less than μ_s. It opposes the direction of sliding.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the coefficient of friction?

options.text: The force of friction divided by the weight of the object
The spring constant of the surface material
A dimensionless ratio of the friction force to the normal force that characterizes the roughness between two surfaces
The angle at which an object begins to slide

quiz_questions.explanation: The coefficient of friction (μ) is a dimensionless number that depends on the nature of both surfaces. μ_s (static) is typically greater than μ_k (kinetic).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the normal force?

options.text: A force that always equals the object's weight
The horizontal component of any contact force
The perpendicular contact force exerted by a surface on an object resting on or pressing against it
The force of gravity on an object

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force is perpendicular to the contact surface. It adjusts to prevent penetration and is NOT always equal to mg -- it depends on other forces acting on the object.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'angle of repose'?

options.text: The angle between the friction force and the normal force
The angle at which an object on an incline just begins to slide, where tanθ = μ_s
The critical angle for total internal reflection
The angle of a banked curve at which no friction is needed

quiz_questions.explanation: The angle of repose is the maximum incline angle before sliding begins. At this angle, mg sinθ = μ_s mg cosθ, so tanθ = μ_s.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is rolling friction?

options.text: The static friction that prevents wheel slip
Friction that causes wheels to turn
The resistance to motion caused by deformation of surfaces when a round object rolls, typically much smaller than sliding friction
Friction proportional to the square of velocity

quiz_questions.explanation: Rolling friction arises from deformation at the contact patch. It is typically much smaller than sliding friction, which is why wheels and bearings are so effective.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does it mean that 'friction is velocity-independent' in the simple model?

options.text: Kinetic friction depends on the object's speed
Friction only acts on stationary objects
Static friction is always greater than kinetic friction regardless of speed
In the basic friction model, kinetic friction f_k = μ_k N does not depend on the sliding speed

quiz_questions.explanation: In the simple Coulomb friction model, f_k = μ_k N is independent of sliding speed. In reality, friction can vary with speed, but this approximation works well for many situations.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between the normal force on a flat surface and on an incline?

options.text: On an incline N = mg sinθ
The normal force is the same in both cases
On a flat surface the normal force is zero
On a flat surface N = mg; on an incline N = mg cosθ (with no other forces)

quiz_questions.explanation: On a flat surface with no other vertical forces, N = mg. On an incline, only the component perpendicular to the surface is balanced: N = mg cosθ.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'fluid friction' or 'drag'?

options.text: The buoyant force on a submerged object
The resistive force experienced by an object moving through a fluid, typically proportional to velocity or velocity squared
Friction between two solid surfaces with lubricant

quiz_questions.explanation: Drag is the resistive force from a fluid (air or liquid). At low speeds, F_drag ∝ v (Stokes drag); at higher speeds, F_drag ∝ v² (turbulent drag).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the role of friction in rolling without slipping?

options.text: Friction is not involved in rolling
Rolling friction propels the object forward
Static friction at the contact point provides the torque needed for rolling and maintains the constraint v = rω
Kinetic friction slows down the rolling object

quiz_questions.explanation: Static friction (not kinetic) acts at the contact point for rolling without slipping. It provides the torque to maintain the rotational-translational constraint v = rω.

quiz_questions.question_text: A delivery truck (mass 5000 kg) must stop on a wet road where μ_k = 0.3. If the truck is traveling at 25 m/s, what is the minimum stopping distance? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: Deceleration a = μ_k g = 0.3(10) = 3 m/s². Stopping distance: d = v²/(2a) = 625/6 ≈ 104 m.

quiz_questions.question_text: A worker pushes a 40 kg crate at constant velocity using a force at 25° below horizontal. If μ_k = 0.35, what pushing force is required? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: N = mg + F sin25°. Friction: f = μ_k(mg + F sin25°). At constant velocity: F cos25° = 0.35(400 + F×0.423). 0.906F = 140 + 0.148F. 0.758F = 140. F ≈ 185 N. With precise values ≈ 174 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: A child (30 kg) slides down a playground slide that is 4 m long at 35° and arrives at the bottom at 3 m/s (starting from rest). What is the coefficient of kinetic friction? (g = 10 m/s²)
A 500 kg race car rounds a flat curve of radius 100 m. The tires have μ_s = 0.9. What is the maximum safe speed? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: Friction provides centripetal force: μ_s mg = mv²/r. v = sqrt(μ_s gr) = sqrt(0.9 × 10 × 100) = sqrt(900) = 30 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A refrigerator (100 kg) sits on a floor with μ_s = 0.5 and μ_k = 0.3. A person pushes horizontally with 400 N. What happens? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: It doesn't move because f_s,max = 500 N

quiz_questions.explanation: Maximum static friction = μ_s mg = 0.5(100)(10) = 500 N. Since the applied force (400 N) < 500 N, the refrigerator does not move.

quiz_questions.question_text: A sled (20 kg) is pulled by a rope at 30° above horizontal across snow (μ_k = 0.1) with a tension of 50 N. What is the sled's acceleration? (g = 10 m/s²)
In a physics experiment, a block is launched up a rough incline (30°, μ_k = 0.2) at 10 m/s. How far up the incline does it travel before stopping? (g = 10 m/s²)
A hockey player shoots a puck (0.17 kg) at 40 m/s across ice (μ_k = 0.05). How far does the puck travel before stopping? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: Deceleration: a = μ_k g = 0.05(10) = 0.5 m/s². d = v²/(2a) = 1600/1 = 1600 m.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car (1200 kg) drives at 20 m/s around a banked curve (bank angle 15°, radius 80 m). What friction force is needed if the speed exceeds the design speed? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: Friction force ≈ 2,832 N directed down the incline
Friction force ≈ 2,832 N directed up the incline
Friction force ≈ 2,880 N directed up the incline (toward center)

quiz_questions.explanation: Design speed: v_d = sqrt(rg tanθ) = sqrt(80(10)(0.268)) = sqrt(214) ≈ 14.6 m/s. Since 20 > 14.6, friction acts up the incline. The centripetal force needed is mv²/r = 1200(400)/80 = 6000 N. N sin15° + f cos15° = 6000 and N cos15° - f sin15° = mg = 12000. Solving gives f ≈ 2880 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: A shipping container (2000 kg) sits in the bed of a truck. The coefficient of static friction between container and truck bed is 0.4. What maximum acceleration can the truck have without the container sliding? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: Static friction accelerates the container: f_s,max = μ_s mg = ma_max. a_max = μ_s g = 0.4(10) = 4 m/s².
